preFix.title: Insulating a cellar ceiling with rigid PIR boards
preFix.subtitle: Fixing insulation to the underside of the ground floor from the cellar
preFix.excerpt: A ground floor over an unheated cellar loses heat at roughly the same rate as an uninsulated suspended timber ground floor, around 1.0 W/m2K or worse. Fixing 70 to 100 mm PIR boards to the underside of the floor joists from the cellar brings the U-value to around 0.20 W/m2K and is far easier than lifting floorboards above.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B 2022 for floors, 0.25 W/m2K existing-dwelling target. CIBSE Guide A floor heat-loss data. Energy Saving Trust floor insulation guidance 2024.

content.text: A cellar under a ground floor gives direct access to the underside of the floor structure, the most convenient possible position for insulating a suspended floor. There is no disruption to the room above, no boards to lift, and the full joist depth is accessible. A 100 mm
PIR board
fixed flat to the underside of the joists (not between them, that requires the thermal bridging of the joists to be addressed separately) achieves a
U-value
of around 0.18 W/m2K.

Eye protection for every cut, every drill. Gloves for handling PIR boards. Do not wear gloves when operating a saw. Identify all services in the cellar ceiling zone before fixing: electrical conduit, water pipes, and heating pipes are the most common. A cable detector should be used before each fixing location.
What you need
Per 10 m2 of floor:
100 mm PIR boards (foil-faced), 12 m2 to allow for waste
PIR adhesive or mechanical fixings (expanding mushroom head plugs into the joists), 50 No.
Foil tape, 2 rolls
Timber battens 50 × 25 mm, for fixing boards to joist soffit (optional support method)
Installation

Measure the floor zone and divide into manageable panels. A standard 1,200 × 2,400 mm PIR board covers 2.88 m2 and weighs 6 to 8 kg. Working overhead in a low cellar is tiring; cut boards to half size (1,200 × 1,200 mm) for easier handling.

Fix battens to the joist soffit at 1,200 mm centres (or to the board panel dimensions) to carry the boards. A batten nailed flush to the bottom of each joist gives a continuous bearing edge along the joist. Alternatively, drive expanding fixings through the board into the joist faces, this requires a temporary prop or a second pair of hands to hold the board while fixing.
Apply a bead of PIR adhesive to the back of the board and lift it into position against the joists. Fix with expanding plugs. Stagger the board joints.
Tape all joints with foil tape, pressed firmly. Fill any gaps at wall edges with cut PIR off-cuts and tape.

The PIR boards will resist small impacts but are not suitable as a walkable ceiling. If the cellar is used for storage and the ceiling will be walked under, add 6 mm or 9 mm plywood below the PIR layer to protect it.
Expected saving

For a 40 m2 ground floor over a cellar, upgrading from uninsulated (U-value 1.0) to 100 mm PIR (0.18) saves roughly 1,600 kWh per year in a typical UK winter with a 10°C mean indoor-to-outdoor temperature difference. Payback on materials is 4 to 5 years.
